title.en: Advocating for the customer against internal pressure
title.es: Defender el interés del cliente frente a la presión interna

question.en: Describe a decision where you advocated for the customer's interest against internal pressure — a deadline, a revenue goal, or a convenience argument. What was the internal argument, and how did you reframe it?
question.es: Describe una decisión en la que defendiste el interés del cliente frente a la presión interna — un plazo, un objetivo de ingresos o un argumento de conveniencia. ¿Cuál era el argumento interno y cómo lo reformulaste?

must_include: the specific internal pressure — who was making the argument, what the business logic was (revenue, speed, scope), and why it was a plausible position, not a straw man
the specific customer harm or risk the candidate identified — named precisely in terms of what the customer would experience, lose, or misunderstand if the internal pressure won
how the candidate reframed the argument — the specific reasoning or evidence used to make the customer interest case to a business audience, not just 'I said we should protect the customer'
the named cost the candidate absorbed or accepted in advocating for the customer — delayed timeline, reduced revenue projection, scope cut, or strained relationship with stakeholders

what held the bar: whether the candidate fell back on policy ('the rules require disclosure') or articulated the underlying principle ('customers making decisions based on misleading terms erodes trust in a way that compounds over time') — the latter is the stronger DTRT signal
must_avoid: framing the customer protection argument as universally obvious — if the internal pressure was genuinely compelling, say so; DTRT requires that the competing argument was worth taking seriously

retroactive ethics: describing a decision that turned out to benefit both customers and revenue as a DTRT story — the signal requires that holding for the customer had a real cost to something internal
blaming the business stakeholders for not caring about customers rather than owning the candidate's role in changing the outcome
